studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were collected at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks post-randomization.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 received access to somnio (digital CBT-I) for 3 months in addition to care-as-usual (CAU). Group 2 continued with CAU and received access to somnio after 6 months (waitlist control group).
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Depression severity measured with PHQ-9 at 12 and 24 weeks post-randomization. Secondary outcomes: Insomnia severity (ISI), daytime sleepiness (ESS), fatigue (FSS), quality of life (WHQOL_BREF), emotional state (PANAS), sleep continuity (sleep diary), self-efficacy (PCS), and emotion regulation (RSQ) at 12 and 24 weeks post-randomization.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were collected at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomization.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1 received digital CBT-I (somnio) and a digital sleep diary for 8 weeks. Group 2 received a digital sleep diary (malio) for 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Primary outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks. Secondary outcomes: Sleep continuity (sleep diary and actigraphy, continuously for 8 weeks), Dysfunctional Beliefs About Sleep (DBAS), Pre-Sleep Arousal (PSAS), Daytime Well-being (WHO-5), Quality of Life (WHQOL_BREF), Symptoms of Anxiety and Depression (HADS).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) were observed, indicating robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital CBT-I arm compared to the control group. Improvements in self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables were also noted.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia delivered via the "Somnio" app in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic. Arm 2: Filling in of sleep-diary in addition to treatment-as-usual in trauma clinic.
studies.collected_data: Posttraumatic symptoms assessed with PCL-5 and PTCI questionnaires. Sleep quality measured with sleep diaries and PSQI. Anxiety measured with GAD-7, depression with QIDS-SR16, and life quality with WHODAS 2.0. Measurements taken prior to intervention, at 10 weeks, and at 6 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: Digital cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) completed over 8 weeks. Control group: Waitlist control group.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: ISI Insomnia Severity Index (Baseline, 8 and 24 weeks). Secondary Outcomes: Pain symptoms (German Pain Questionnaire, Multidimensional Pain Inventory), Daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale), Fatigue (Fatigue Severity Scale), Dysfunctional attitudes to sleep (Opinions About Sleep Questionnaire), Well-being (WHO-5 Well-Being Index), Quality of life (WHO Quality of Life WHOQOL_BREF), Psychological need satisfaction (Basic Psychological Need Satisfaction and Frustration Scale), Dreams and nightmares (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire, Nightmare Distress Questionnaire), Depression and anxiety (General Depression Scale, State-Trait Anxiety Inventory), Analysis of motivational schemas (Questionnaire for Analysis of Motivational Schemas, Incongruence Questionnaire). All measured at baseline, 8 weeks, and 24 weeks.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention consisted of 11 modules completed over 6-8 weeks, with follow-ups at 6 and 12 months.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received digital cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) consisting of 11 modules completed over 6-8 weeks. Control group was a waiting list control group.
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: ISI Insomnia Severity Index (pre, post, follow-up 6 months, follow-up 12 months). Secondary Outcomes: DBAS-16 Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes About Sleep, FSS Fatigue Severity Index, ESS Epworth Sleepiness Scale, CES-D Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (short version), STAI-T State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, WHOQOL_BREF WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire (brief version), WHO-5 WHO-Five Well-Being Index, MADRE Mannheim Dream Questionnaire, DTI Düsseldorf Dream Inventory, NDQ Nightmare Distress Questionnaire, CAN Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares (all measured pre, post, follow-up 6 months, follow-up 12 months).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At post-treatment, dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in ISI scores (Diffadj = -7.60) compared to the control group (d = -2.08). Clinical improvements were observed in responder and remission rates, daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, and anxiety (effect sizes d = 0.26–1.02). Long-term effects were sustained in the intervention group (effect sizes d = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received digital CBT-I via a mobile app or web platform; control group was a waiting list control (WLC).
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) for depressive symptoms, insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, mechanistic effect measures.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in depressive symptoms (-3.34 and -2.83; ps <0.001; ds = 0.66-0.78) in the dCBT-I group. Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46-1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33-1.14). Effects remained stable across a heterogeneous sample.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: dCBT-I group received digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia; WLC group was a waitlist control condition.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), sleep-related symptoms, pain-related symptoms, measures of well-being, measures of dreams, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in insomnia severity compared to WLC both after 8 weeks (-4.36, p < 0.001; d = -1.18) and 24 weeks (-4.88, p < 0.001; d = -1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved (ds = 0.22-0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were observed for secondary outcomes, including daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47-1.12).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received 8 weeks of digital cognitive behavioural therapy. Control group received 8 weeks of digital sleep monitoring via a self-monitoring application.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia severity (assessed at baseline, 8 weeks, and 16 weeks post-randomisation), sleep diaries, actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects (d = 0.87-1.08) were observed, with significant reductions in insomnia severity (-3.70 and -2.97 at 8 and 16 weeks, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy group compared to the control group. Improvements in self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables were also noted.
